class_schedule.topic: The Replication Crisis and Open Science
learning_objectives: Describe the replication crisis in psychology and its causes
Explain open science practices: preregistration, open data, open materials
Evaluate the methodological reforms proposed by the credibility revolution
readings: Open Science Collaboration (2015), 'Estimating the Reproducibility of Psychological Science'; Nosek et al., 'Promoting an open research culture'

lecture_notes.introduction: The replication crisis revealed that many published findings in psychology fail to replicate. This seminar examines the crisis, its causes, and the open science movement's response.
key_concepts: Reproducibility Project: only 39% of 100 studies replicated
Causes: publication bias, p-hacking, HARKing, small samples, QRPs
Preregistration: specifying hypotheses and analyses before data collection
Registered reports: peer review before data collection
Open data, open materials, and reproducible analysis pipelines
lecture_notes.demonstration: Preregister a study: students write a preregistration for a hypothetical experiment using the OSF template

review.value: Journals preferentially publish significant results, creating a distorted literature where effects appear larger and more reliable than they are
Publicly committing to hypotheses and analysis plan before data collection prevents p-hacking and HARKing; distinguishes confirmatory from exploratory analysis

learning_objectives: Describe when to use multilevel modeling, structural equation modeling, and mediation analysis
Explain the concept of statistical power and conduct a power analysis
Discuss effect sizes, confidence intervals, and their advantages over p-values
readings: Cumming, 'The New Statistics: Why and How' (Psychological Science, 2014)

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern psychological research requires statistical methods beyond the basics. This session surveys advanced techniques and promotes a shift from significance testing to estimation thinking.
key_concepts: Effect sizes as the primary focus of research: d, r, odds ratio
Confidence intervals: what they are and what they are not
Power analysis: determining sample size before data collection (G*Power)
Multilevel modeling for nested data (students within classrooms)
Mediation and moderation analysis: when and how to use
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a power analysis using G*Power for an independent-samples t-test with expected d = 0.50

review.value: Cumming: emphasize effect sizes and confidence intervals over NHST p-values; report exact p-values rather than p < .05; preregister studies
Determining the minimum sample size needed to detect an expected effect with a given probability (typically 80%); prevents underpowered studies that produce unreliable results

learning_objectives: Describe major qualitative approaches: phenomenology, grounded theory, thematic analysis
Explain the criteria for rigor in qualitative research
Evaluate when mixed methods designs add value over single-method studies
readings: Braun & Clarke, 'Using Thematic Analysis in Psychology' (2006); Creswell, Research Design, Ch. 10

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all important questions in psychology can be answered with numbers. Qualitative methods provide depth, context, and participant voice that quantitative methods cannot capture.
key_concepts: Epistemological foundations: positivism vs. constructivism vs. pragmatism
Thematic analysis: six-phase process (Braun & Clarke)
Grounded theory: building theory from data (Glaser & Strauss)
Rigor in qualitative research: credibility, transferability, dependability, confirmability
Mixed methods designs: convergent, explanatory sequential, exploratory sequential
lecture_notes.demonstration: Practice thematic analysis: code a brief interview transcript and identify preliminary themes

review.value: Braun & Clarke's six phases: familiarization, generating codes, searching for themes, reviewing themes, defining themes, writing up
Using multiple methods, data sources, or researchers to converge on findings; strengthens credibility in both qualitative and mixed methods research

learning_objectives: Describe the process of conducting a systematic review
Explain meta-analytic procedures: effect size calculation, heterogeneity, moderator analysis
Evaluate the strengths and limitations of meta-analysis
readings: Borenstein et al., Introduction to Meta-Analysis, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses sit at the top of the evidence hierarchy. They synthesize entire literatures into quantitative summaries, but they are only as good as the primary studies they include.
key_concepts: PRISMA guidelines for systematic review reporting
Effect size calculation: converting different statistics to a common metric
Fixed-effects vs. random-effects models
Heterogeneity assessment: Q statistic, I-squared
Publication bias detection: funnel plots, trim-and-fill, p-curve analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate a weighted mean effect size from five studies and assess heterogeneity using provided data

review.value: Assumes studies estimate different but related effect sizes; appropriate when studies vary in methods, populations, and contexts (usual case in psychology)
Graph of effect size vs. precision (1/SE); asymmetry suggests publication bias (missing small, non-significant studies)

class_schedule.topic: Research Proposal Workshop I: Literature Review and Hypotheses
learning_objectives: Develop a focused, theoretically grounded research question
Write a targeted literature review identifying a clear gap
Formulate specific, testable hypotheses with predicted effect sizes
readings: Selected readings based on individual proposal topics

lecture_notes.introduction: This session begins the hands-on development of your original research proposal. Moving from interest area to research question to testable hypothesis is the most challenging part of the research process.
key_concepts: From topic to question: narrowing through literature review
Identifying gaps: what is unknown, contradictory, or methodologically limited
Theoretical grounding: every hypothesis should follow from a theory or prior findings
Predicted effect sizes: estimate from prior research or theoretical reasoning
Feasibility assessment: sample availability, measures, timeline
lecture_notes.demonstration: Peer feedback workshop: present research questions and hypotheses for constructive critique

review.value: The best research questions arise from contradictions in the literature, untested boundary conditions, or methodological improvements on existing studies
Estimating the expected effect size before data collection is essential for power analysis and demonstrates understanding of the phenomenon

class_schedule.topic: Research Proposal Workshop II: Methods and Analysis Plan
learning_objectives: Design a rigorous methodology with appropriate controls
Select valid and reliable measures with psychometric justification
Develop a detailed preregistered analysis plan including power analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: The method section is where research quality is determined. A well-designed study with clear preregistered analyses produces credible findings regardless of the results.
key_concepts: Sampling: power-based sample size determination
Measurement selection: reliability, validity, cultural appropriateness for target population
Experimental controls: random assignment, blinding, manipulation checks
Preregistered analysis plan: primary analysis, secondary analyses, decision rules
Contingency planning: what if assumptions are violated or recruitment is difficult?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Method section workshop: peer review of draft method sections with structured feedback

review.value: Specify: primary analysis (hypothesis test), secondary analyses (exploratory), decision rules (what constitutes support vs. refutation), and how assumption violations will be handled
A measure confirming that the independent variable manipulation was experienced as intended; essential for interpreting null results

quizzes.topic: Open Science, Advanced Methods, and Qualitative Research

questions.question: A researcher runs a study with 30 participants per group, finds p = .048, and publishes the result. Explain three reasons why this finding may not replicate, using concepts from the replication crisis literature.
questions.answer: (1) Low statistical power: with n = 30 per group, power to detect a medium effect (d = 0.50) is only about 50%. Underpowered significant results have inflated effect sizes (winner's curse) and low positive predictive value. (2) p-value close to threshold: p = .048 suggests the evidence is barely significant and highly sensitive to minor analytical choices (researcher degrees of freedom). A slight change in outlier handling or variable coding could push it past .05. (3) Publication bias: this result was published because it was significant. If 10 similar studies were conducted and only this one found p < .05, it represents a false positive selected from the file drawer of non-significant results.

rubric.key: Identifies low power and its consequences
Addresses marginal p-value and researcher degrees of freedom
Discusses publication bias / file drawer problem

questions.question: Compare thematic analysis with grounded theory. When would you choose each approach, and how do they differ in their relationship to existing theory?
questions.answer: Thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke) is a flexible method for identifying patterns (themes) in qualitative data. It can be used atheoretically (data-driven) or theoretically (guided by existing frameworks). Choose it when you want to describe patterns in data related to a specific research question, especially when working within an existing theoretical framework. Grounded theory (Glaser & Strauss) aims to generate new theory from data through iterative coding, constant comparison, and theoretical sampling. Choose it when no adequate existing theory explains the phenomenon and your goal is theory building. Key difference: thematic analysis can work within existing theory; grounded theory deliberately sets aside prior theory to let new theory emerge from the data.

rubric.key: Identifies correct conditions for choosing each
Explains the key difference in relationship to theory

questions.question: A meta-analysis of 50 studies on a therapy yields a mean effect size of d = 0.55, but I-squared = 82%. Interpret these results and explain what the high heterogeneity means for the conclusion.
questions.answer: The mean effect size d = 0.55 suggests a medium-to-large overall treatment effect. However, I-squared = 82% indicates that 82% of the variance in effect sizes across studies is due to true differences between studies (heterogeneity), not sampling error. This means the therapy does not produce a consistent effect across contexts. The overall mean is not very informative because studies differ dramatically in their results. The researcher should conduct moderator analyses to identify what explains the heterogeneity (e.g., therapy duration, client severity, therapist training, outcome measure). The practical implication: the therapy works well in some conditions but not others, and identifying those conditions is more important than the overall average.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets I-squared = 82% as high heterogeneity
Recommends moderator analysis and explains why overall mean is limited

questions.question: You plan to test whether a mindfulness intervention reduces test anxiety. Design the study using best practices from the seminar: specify the design, sample size justification, primary outcome measure, and one element of your preregistered analysis plan.
questions.answer: Design: Randomized controlled trial with two groups (mindfulness intervention vs. active control -- relaxation training). Random assignment minimizes confounds; an active control rules out non-specific effects (attention, expectancy). Sample size: Assume medium effect (d = 0.50) based on prior mindfulness meta-analyses, alpha = .05, power = .80. G*Power: n = 64 per group (128 total). Recruit 150 to allow for 15% attrition. Primary outcome: Test Anxiety Inventory (TAI; Spielberger), administered pre-intervention, post-intervention, and 1-month follow-up. TAI has alpha > .90 and strong criterion validity. Preregistered analysis: Primary analysis is a 2 (group) x 3 (time) mixed ANOVA on TAI scores. Significant group x time interaction followed by planned contrasts at each time point. Decision rule: reject null if p < .05 for the interaction and the intervention group shows greater reduction than control at post-test.

rubric.key: Appropriate design with active control and randomization
Valid outcome measure with psychometric justification
Specific preregistered analysis with decision rule

major_assignments.title: Original Research Proposal
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete, preregistered original research proposal on a topic in psychology. The proposal must include a literature review, theoretical framework, specific hypotheses, detailed methodology with power analysis, preregistered analysis plan, and discussion of anticipated results and limitations.
objectives: Formulate an original, theoretically grounded research question
Design a rigorous methodology following open science best practices
Conduct a power analysis and justify sample size
Write a proposal meeting the standards of a preregistered study
deliverables: G*Power output for sample size justification

criteria: Specific, testable hypotheses with predicted effect sizes
Addresses threats to internal and external validity
Comprehensive literature review with clear gap identification
Rigorous methodology with power analysis and appropriate controls
Complete preregistered analysis plan with decision rules

major_assignments.title: Peer Review of Research Proposal
major_assignments.description: Conduct a detailed peer review of a classmate's research proposal, modeled on the journal peer review process. Evaluate the theoretical rationale, methodology, analysis plan, and overall feasibility. Provide constructive recommendations for improvement.
objectives: Apply methodological knowledge to critically evaluate research proposals
Provide constructive, specific feedback following journal review standards
Identify both strengths and weaknesses in research design
Practice the peer review skills essential for academic careers
deliverables: Structured peer review (1,500 words) following journal review format
Summary recommendation: accept, revise, or redesign
Specific suggestions for improving each section

criteria: Rigorous critical evaluation of all proposal sections

seminar_participation.description: Active engagement in discussions, presentations, and workshops

proposal_presentation.description: Oral defense of research proposal to class

final_portfolio.description: Revised proposal incorporating peer feedback with reflection on revisions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for this seminar-format course. More than 2 absences will result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late proposals are penalized 10% per day. Peer reviews must be submitted on time for the feedback cycle.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback is a core component. Research proposals must be independently developed and written.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Proposals must be original work. Plagiarism will result in course failure and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to OSF, G*Power, SPSS or R, university library databases, and reference management software.
